By now you've learned a fair bit. You may have noticed that some systems seem to do things "automatically". So now we'll introduce scheduled task execution (crontab).

Do you have habits — things you do at fixed times every day? Like brushing your teeth and washing your face every morning, or before bed every night. These are things you do daily at fixed times — that's a kind of work schedule.

Hard to feel? That example was too everyday. Let me try another. Say you're a company owner. Every year you might need to organize team-building events, employee trips, planning meetings, year-end bonus distribution, etc. There's a lot to remember, and the human brain can't easily track when each thing should happen. So you'd put these on Google Calendar, and Google Calendar reminds you at the set times — that's also a work schedule.
Why bring up work scheduling? Because in real-world dev, we use this technique. So this post covers how to implement Crontab functionality in Node.js.
Side note — Linux has two scheduling tools:
is more commonly used, so we cover that. Linux is a free, open-source UNIX-like operating system.
You might think we can just dive into Crontab now?
Nope — you need to learn the Crontab format first. The format is a really important concept.
language-bash shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
Don't worry, I'm not pranking you. This is the actual Crontab format, divided into five parts:
For the example above, it means "every minute, every hour, every day, every month, every week" — meaning it runs every minute.
Say it's 2023/08/31 21:12 right now. This Crontab would run at:
If we want to run every half hour:
If we want to run at 12:00 every day:
A more specific case — at 3 PM on the 1st of every month:
What about specific dates? Crontab can do that too — for example, the annual Christmas:
Pretty cool right? What if every Wednesday at 9 AM?
Crontab's week starts at 0. 0 is Sunday, 1 is Monday, etc.
What if every Monday through Friday at 9 AM?
Super interesting right? Crontab is really flexible — you can configure based on your needs. Crontab has special symbols too:
So Crontab is also called the "crontab schedule expression" — its writing style is similar to expressions. Here's a simple diagram:
Crontab also has special symbols like
, which means run when the system boots. Usage is simple — say we originally wrote:
If we want it to run on system boot:
: runs once a year on January 1st at 00:00 — same as
: runs once a month on the 1st at 00:00 — same as
: runs once a week on Sunday at 00:00 — same as
: runs every hour at minute 00 — same as
So on Linux, how do you execute your command? Easy — just append the command to execute after the crontab time format:
I won't cover Linux in detail — that's not the focus of this series.
If you find Crontab format hard to memorize, you can test on
— it parses Crontab format and tells you when it'll run.
That's a wrap, see you in the next one.
